data.title: U.S. Fed Keeps Interest Rates On Hold As Economic Outlook Improves
data.description:
The U.S. Federal Reserve decided to keep its key overnight interest rate on hold at its first policy meeting of 2026. Sam Chai, Vice President, Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, breaks down the Fed’s decision and explains why he expects policymakers to start cutting again in the second half of the year.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie - The US Federal Reserve has put a pause on interest rates, keeping them at a range between 3.5% and 3.75%. Joining us for more is Sam Chai of TD Asset Management. And Sam, when you look at today's announcements, did anything really jump out at you?

Sam Chai - So I would say that today's decision to -- for the Fed to hold is a pretty widely expected decision, for a few good reasons. For one, the Fed has already delivered three consecutive cuts over the past meetings. Policy rate now is currently at 3.75%. And here I quote Chair Powell is now "within the range" of estimates of where the neutral rate is. So there's no argument that it's now much less restrictive, so a good place for the Fed to wait and see a bit.

On the labor market front, we have been seeing some a bit more stabilization in data of late. So December job growth was around 50,000, which is a solid data point. Unemployment rate actually went down slightly as well, so it's a good sign. And these are acknowledged in the latest statement as well.

Finally, on the inflation front, continued to be steady, I would say. Core CPI is around 2.5% to 3% range, so still above the Fed's 2% target but nothing too concerning for the Fed as well. So overall, there is no change to forward guidance from the Fed. And expectation is that we could continue to see a holding pattern in the near months.

There are two dovish dissents today from both Governor Waller and Miran, not unexpected by the market. There is this tiny dovish cohort, but more importantly, the bulk of the committee is still with the Chair Powell and prefer to be in a holding pattern right now.

Anthony Okolie - So given the fact that the economy is somewhat resilient right now, what's the likelihood that we could see a rate cut later this year?

Sam Chai - So I think the starting place would be, first, for us to revisit where the Fed's forecast of long-term neutral rate to be. And that is currently around 3%, meaning that there is still some room for policy rate to go lower before the Fed starts to be concerned about refueling upside inflation risk by the monetary policies. And so with that in mind, I see there are two paths for us to see more rate cuts this year. The first path is a bad news rate cuts. By that, I mean if labor market suddenly deteriorates further in the coming months, we could see rate cuts as early as next meeting or even the meeting after the next, as we know the Fed wants to continue to secure the soft landing for the US.

On the other hand, there could also be good news cut. The Fed continued to expect that inflation will slightly pick up in the coming months. But around end of first half, beginning of second half, we would expect the bulk of the tariff pass-through inflation effect to already materialize. So if inflation at that time is still relatively reasonable, then it opens up room for the Fed to cut further at that time, and on good inflation news. So considering both possibilities, I do see there's a decent chance we see several cuts by the Fed by the end of this year.

Anthony Okolie - All right. So, given that, let's talk about bond yields and what they're telling us about Fed policy, where Fed policy could be headed, especially given the uncertainty around a new leadership for the Fed as well as the pending Supreme Court decision on tariffs. What are bond yields telling us right now?

Sam Chai - So the US rates market currently priced in the first rate cut around June to July and two full cuts by the end of this year. And it's no coincidence that the market has priced in the first rate cut by June, because that is also when we expect, to your point, the new Fed chair to come to take the reins for the FOMC meetings. And market does expect that the new Fed chair will be shifting towards a more dovish reaction function, as well.

On the second point on Supreme Court rulings, so the Supreme Court ruling is expected to be against the Trump administration's on tariffs. Polymarket currently assigned 70% odds for that. But the market is not naive, right? We know the White House is likely going to find alternative channels to implement their tariff policy. So in the near term, inflation risks remain to the upside. Hence why the market thinks that it's likely the Fed will be mindful of this and be in a holding pattern for the first half and then start to cut in the second half of this year.

Anthony Okolie - I want to switch to fiscal policy because we've got the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which is making its way through the system this year. How might that influence the Fed's thinking on rate cuts down the road?

Sam Chai - So based on the range of estimates for the effect of the One Big Beautiful Bill, expectations for a roughly, call it, 0.3% to 0.5% contribution to US GDP growth this year.

Anthony Okolie - So there could be a bit of a tailwind there?

Sam Chai - That is correct, from consumer stimulus and CapEx stimulus. In particular, on the consumer stimulus front, it amounts to over $100 billion. And it's expected to be pretty front-loaded and acutely felt in the first half of this year, which is another reason why the market -- this is another reason for why the market expects that we can be in a holding pattern because of the support for the economy in the first half. And we see perhaps more easing in the second half of this year.

Anthony Okolie - Okay. Let's talk about the US dollar, which has had a pretty tough year. It's still under pressure against the basket of currencies. Where do you see the dollar going from here?

Sam Chai - So very interesting question, maybe a bit of context in the US dollar trend, as well. So in recent months, US dollar has actually been in a wide consolidation range after falling materially in the first half of last year. But in the past two weeks, there has been a lot of volatility, to your point, and downside pressure, as well.

For one, rising geopolitical and policy rates from the US side has refueled this de-dollarization trend. For one, there's also concerns about a potential US-Japan joint intervention, as well. We also have a rising US government shutdown risk in the moment, too. And yesterday, President Trump also commented that he doesn't worry too much about a weak dollar. But then, just today, Treasury Secretary Bessent mentioned that the US still prefers a strong-dollar policy, so a lot of dollar volatility there, as you can imagine.

Overall, I would say that we are sympathetic to this bearish dollar theme. And over the medium term, we do see that the risk is skewed to the downside for US dollar on the expectation that we could see further US dollar hedging flows as well as possibly a dovish shift in Fed reaction function in later this year.

Anthony Okolie - Now, as you mentioned, there's a lot of noise out there. What are the key indicators that you'll be keeping an eye on in the next weeks and months ahead as the Fed considers its next move?

Sam Chai - I think top of mind would be just the nomination of the new Fed chair, who will be selected, and also, if there's any new messages from the Fed chair because the market is keenly monitoring any shift in Fed reaction functions going forward. On the economic data front, on the inflation side, expectation is for a gradual rise in inflation, possibly from more tariff pass-throughs. So very important, in my opinion, to monitor the core goods component, which is most sensitive to tariff-induced inflation.

So if we are seeing a meaningful reacceleration of core goods prices, that would be a major unwelcome surprise for both the market as well as the Federal Reserve. And they will really restrain the Fed's ability to dial down policy rates further.

On the labor market front, we continue to be in this low-firing, low-hiring regime. So very important to monitor data, including initial jobless claims, layoff data, because if we start to see a consistent pickup in these, these will be meaningful red flags and, again, a key risk for the market because both the Fed and the market continue to expect a benign macro backdrop right now and is not pricing in that scenario.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nickel Miners News For The Month Of January 2026
data.description:
Welcome to the nickel miners news for January.

The past month saw nickel prices significantly higher and LME inventory significantly higher.

Nickel price news

As of January 29, the nickel spot price was USD 18,773/t, significantly higher from USD 15,760/tonne last month.

Nickel inventory at the London Metals Exchange [LME] was significantly higher the past month at 286,338 tonnes (255,186 tonnes last month).

Nickel spot price 10 year chart - Current price = USD 18,773/t (source)

Nickel demand v supply chart

S&P Global Market Intelligence forecast nickel oversupply to 2027, then a 'primary' nickel deficit starting in 2028 (as of June 2024) (source)

Carbon Credits forecasts a nickel oversupply to 2030, then growing deficits from 2030-2035 (as of May 2025) (source)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (IEA)

Lithium & Nickel are the two 'high importance to energy' critical materials in the medium term (2025-2035) (source - page 29)

Nickel Market News

On January 7 Bloomberg reported:

Nickel spikes more than 10% as Chinese buying fuels metals surge... Prices for nickel hit a high of $18,785 a ton on the London Metal Exchange, extending a scorching run that has lifted prices nearly 30% since mid-December...While the nickel market is heavily oversupplied, mounting risks to production in top supplier Indonesia have helped shore up sentiment, alongside a broad-based flood of investment in China’s domestic metals markets.

On January 7 Fastmarkets reported: "Nickel prices surge on fears of Indonesian mining quotas."

On January 15 S&P Global announced:

Asian nickel market faces Q1 pressure as oversupply outweighs output cuts...S&P Global Energy CERA projects that the nickel market will remain in surplus by 179,000 metric tons through 2026, before transitioning to a deficit of 2,000 mt by 2032.

On January 19 IDN reported:

Indonesia cuts 2026 nickel quota, global prices jump immediately...the Ministry of Energy and Mineral Resources (ESDM) has set the 2026 nickel ore production target under the Work Plan and Budget (RKAB) at just 250–260 million tonnes. This represents a drop of nearly 34% compared with the 2025 RKAB target of 379 million tonnes...The government said the quota reduction was aimed at aligning mining output with the capacity of domestic processing facilities and smelters.

Nickel Company News

Producers

Norilsk Nickel ("Nornickel")

On January 28 Nornickel announced:

Consolidated production results for FY2025...In 2025, nickel, copper and palladium output came in line with the annual production guidance,while platinum output was ahead of it...In 4Q 2025, consolidated nickel output increased 9% quarter-on-quarter (q-o-q) to 58 kt, as the equipment recovered its capacity utilization rate to normal after the scheduled maintenance carried out in 3Q25. Practically all of the metal were produced from the Company’s own Russian feed (57.9 kt).In 2025, consolidated nickel output decreased 3% to 199 kt, as a result of higher mined volumes of disseminated ore and lower ore volumes of rich ore. All of the metal were produced from the Company’s own Russian feed (198.2 kt), which is slightly above the production target range of 196-204 kt.

Nornickel 2026 production guidance - Nickel 193-203kt (source)

Vale SA (NYSE:VALE)

Vale Voisey's Bay Mine is a key Canadian and global source of nickel. Vale plans a nickel sulphate refinery at Bécancour in Québec to supply GM with battery grade nickel sulfate with deliveries targeted to commence in H2 2026, subject to various regulatory approvals.

On January 2 Bloomberg reported:

Vale Indonesia halts nickel mining on delayed work plan approval...Despite the halt, approval is expected soon and the delay is unlikely to impact overall operational sustainability, the joint venture between Vale SA’s base metals unit and the Indonesian government said in a filing.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (GLCNF)

On January 8 Glencore announced:

Statement regarding Rio Tinto. Glencore notes recent media speculation and confirms that it is in preliminary discussions with Rio Tinto plc and Rio Tinto Limited (together, “Rio Tinto”) about a possible combination of some or all of their businesses, which could include an all-share merger between Rio Tinto and Glencore.

On January 29 Glencore announced:

Full Year 2025 Production Report...Adjusting for 5,000 tonnes of Koniambo production in the base period (prior to its transition to care and maintenance), own sourced nickel production of 71,900 tonnes was 5,400 tonnes (7%) lower than 2024, reflecting lower production at both INO and Murrin Murrin.

Chart showing Glencore's 2025 production and 2026 guidance (source)

BHP Group [ASX:BHP] (NYSE:BHP)

BHP's Nickel West operations include the Mt Keith Nickel Mine in Australia. Nickel West produced 81.6 kt of nickel in the '24 financial year. In 2024 BHP temporarily suspended Western Australia Nickel operations due to low nickel prices. Stage 1 production of the Kwinana Nickel Refinery is aimed to be 100ktpa nickel sulphate.

On January 20 BHP announced:

BHP Operational Review for the half year ended 31 December 2025...“BHP delivered another half of very strong performance with operational records at our copper and iron ore assets"...

Note: Nickel production was zero due to the suspension of WA nickel operations.

Anglo American [LSX:AAL] (OTCPK:AAUKF) - Planned merger with Teck to form Anglo Teck plc

Anglo American's sale of their nickel business to China's MMG Singapore Resources (update here on the sale delay).

No news for the month.

Eramet [FRA:ERA] (OTCPK:ERMAY)

No significant news for the month; however Reuters reported in Dec. 2025: "Eramet eyes manganese, lithium growth in turnaround plan."

Sherritt International (OTCPK:SHERF)[TSX:S]

On January 19 Sherritt International announced:

Sherritt reports 2025 production results and Operational Update... Finished nickel and cobalt production were at the lower ends of their revised 2025 guidance ranges. In the fourth quarter 2025, continued lower-than-expected production of mixed sulphides at Moa impacted feed availability at the refinery.

Sherritt International production for full year 2025 (source)

IGO Limited [ASX:IGO] (OTC:IIDDY)

IGO state on their website: "In June 2024, Cosmos was transitioned into care and maintenance. IGO intends to protect the optionality to restart Cosmos in the future." Forrestania to enter care & maintenance before end CY24. The Nova Mine is their flagship and low cost nickel mine with limited mine life remaining. IGO also has large lithium JV assets.

On January 29 IGO announced:

Quarterly Report - Period ended 31 December 2025. Strong operational result at Nova, first ore processed at Greenbushes CGP3...Nova performed well in quarter. Production and costs continue to track favourably to the remaining life of mine plan...Nova performed well in the quarter, delivering 3.8kt of nickel and 1.8kt of copper.

Note: Nova's nickel production YTD for the financial year is 7,219t.

Jinchuan Group (owns ~60% of Jinchuan Group International Resources [HKEX:2362])

On December 29 Tip Ranks reported: "Jinchuan Group International signs new three-year connected mining services deal with parent JCG."

Sumitomo Metal Mining Co. (OTCPK:SMMYY) ("SMM")

On January 14 SMM announced: "IR-Day 2025 “Advanced Material Business”".

Nickel 28 Capital Corp. [TSXV:NKL] [GR:3JC]

Nickel 28 holds an 8.56% JV interest in Ramu which is operated by the Metallurgical Corporation of China (“MCC”).

On January 7 Nickel 28 announced:

...proposed normal course issuer bid (“NCIB”) to repurchase its common shares...of up to 7,050,819 of its common shares, representing approximately 8.1% of the 86,977,221 common shares comprising Nickel 28’s total issued and outstanding common shares as of January 5, 2026.

Nickel Industries Limited [ASX:NIC] (NICMF)

On January 19 Nickel Industries announced:

Operating Update. December quarter trading update – Adjusted EBITDA from operations expected to be in the range of US$35m-US$40m. Delayed issuance of an increased RKAB for 2025 resulted in significantly reduced Hengjaya Mine ore sales and EBITDA.

Note: The RKAB refers to the Indonesian Government nickel quota.

On January 29 Nickel Industries announced:

Quarterly Activities Report. US$37.3m adjusted EBITDA from operations. Mining operations impacted by RKAB delay. Strong HPAL contribution.

On January 30 Nickel Industries announced:

Completion of ENC 10% acquisition by strategic partner. Completion of Sphere’s acquisition of a 10% interest in the ENC project, at a US$2.4 billion valuation. Sphere is a key accredited supplier to SpaceX, and its investment represents a strong external validation of both NIC and ENC as global showcases for sustainable, high-quality, low-carbon nickel production...NIC will acquire a final additional 2% interest in ENC, increasing its shareholding from 44% to 46% and becoming the largest shareholder in the project.

Note: NIC refers to Nickel Industries.

Other nickel producers

Tsingshan Holding Group Co Ltd (private, China's largest nickel producer, Chinese owned stainless steel producer with large nickel mines in Indonesia), First Quantum Minerals [TSX:FM] (FQVLF), Franco/Nevada [TSX:FNV], MMG [HK:1208], South32 [ASX:S32], Lundin Mining [TSX:LUN], Nickel Asia Corporation [PSE:NIKL], Pacific Nickel Mines [ASX:PNM](PCFNF), Platinum Group Metals’ [TSX:PTM] (PLG).

Nickel juniors

Talon Metals [TSX:TLO] (TLOFF)

Tamarack is a high grade nickel-copper-cobalt project located in Minnesota, USA, with considerable exploration upside. Talon Metals owns a 51% project share, with potential to further earn-in to a 60% share by March 14, 2027. Also 100% of Lundin Mining’s Eagle copper Mine & Humboldt Mill Operations in USA.

On January 9 Talon Metals announced:

Talon Metals completes previously announced acquisition of Lundin Mining’s Eagle Mine and Humboldt Mill Operations creating a multi-asset U.S. nickel-copper company.

On January 27 Talon Metals announced: "Completion of share consolidation... one post-consolidation Talon Share for every ten pre-consolidation."

QPM Energy Limited [ASX:QPM]

QPM is developing the 100% owned Townsville Energy Chemicals Hub ("TECH") battery metals refinery Project. QPM has secured binding offtake agreements for the sale of nickel and cobalt with LG Energy Solutions, POSCO and General Motors. They also have the revenue producing Moranbah Gas Project (“MGP”), which captures waste mine gas from operating coal mines as well as its own petroleum leases.

On January 30 QPM Energy announced:

December 2025 Quarterly Report. Continued progress towards development of the 112MW Isaac Power Station (“IPS”)...TECH Project Grant Funding and Project Update. During the quarter, QPM successfully completed high acid concentration leach testwork...The completion of this testwork is the first milestone to facilitate a $2 million payment under the State Government funding facility ($8 million total). QPM is now in the process of undertaking the next stage of test work.

NexMetals Mining Corp. [TSXV:NEXM] (NEXM)

NexMetals Mining has multi-metal projects in Canada (Post Creek and Halcyon) and Botswana (Selebi Main and Selebi North).

On January 15 NexMetals Mining announced:

NexMetals outlines 2026 Technical Work Programs to advance Selebi and Selkirk deposits in Botswana and accelerates CEO transition.

On January 27 NexMetals Mining announced:

NexMetals drills 32.45 metres of 4.61% CuEq including 5.88% CuEq over 9.65 metres showing continuity of high-grade mineralization at Selebi North..Next Steps. Update the Mineral Resource Estimate (“MRE“) with the 2025 resource expansion drillholes and updated metallurgical test results.

Note: The drill result of 4.61% Cu Eq. was made from 1.61% Cu and 1.46% Ni.

Canada Nickel Company [TSXV:CNC](CNIKF)

On January 12 Canada Nickel announced:

46% increase in Measured & Indicated Resource at Reid Nickel Sulphide Project.

  • Measured & Indicated Resource: Increased 46% to 2.1 million tonnes contained nickel (0.87 billion tonnes @ 0.23% Ni), including a higher grade domain of 0.77 billion tonnes @ 0.25% Ni.
  • Inferred Resource: Increased 47% to 3.2 million tonnes contained nickel (1.45 billion tonnes @ 0.22% Ni), including a higher-grade domain of 0.98 billion tonnes @ 0.24% Ni.
  • Exploration Target1: Potential for an additional 0.5–1.4 billion tonnes @ 0.21–0.22% Ni.

On January 13 Canada Nickel announced:

Crawford Nickel Project named under Ontario’s One Project, One Process Framework. The 1P1P framework is designed to better coordinate Ontario’s permitting and review processes for major mining developments by aligning timelines, responsibilities, and information sharing across provincial ministries.

On January 26 Canada Nickel announced: "Ausenco as lead engineering consultant for detailed engineering of the Crawford Nickel Sulphide Project."

Ardea Resources [ASX:ARL] (ARRRF)

In total, Ardea has 6.1Mt of contained nickel and 386,000t of contained cobalt. They own the KNP Project near Kalgoorlie in Western Australia and have a JV consortium agreement to earn into 50% share of the KNP – Goongarrie Hub with Sumitomo Metal Mining and Mitsubishi Corporation). Ardea is also exploring for gold and nickel sulphide on their >5,100 km2 of 100% controlled tenements in the Eastern Goldfields region of Western Australia.

On January 30 Ardea Resources announced:

December 2025 Quarterly Activities Report.

  • Major Project Status for the KNP and its Goongarrie Hub was renewed for a further three-year term by the Australian Federal Government1, highlighting the Critical Mineral strategic importance of the KNP.
  • As of 31 December 2025, Ardea remains in a strong financial position with $11.6M cash on hand and no debt. The current DFS is being solely funded by the Japanese Consortium (JC) to A$98.5M2.
  • Progress continued on the Mineral Resource Estimate (MRE) in support of the updated mining schedule optimisation and Ore Reserve...
  • DFS metallurgical testwork program results and interpretation are being finalised.
  • Drafting of the DFS report chapters progressed.

The DFS has advanced substantially through CY2025, with several key design improvements, and it remains on schedule for completion in the first half of CY2026.

Centaurus Metals Limited [ASX:CTM] (CTTZF)

Centaurus Metals is an Australian-based minerals exploration company focused on the near-term development of the Jaguar Nickel Sulphide Project, in Northern Brazil.

On January 28 Centaurus Metals announced:

DECEMBER 2025 QUARTERLY ACTIVITIES REPORT. JAGUAR NICKEL SULPHIDE PROJECT, BRAZIL.

  • Mining lease granted in October 2025.
  • Basic engineering commenced and is progressing well with CPC Engineering in Australia to enable the necessary project definition and engineering design information for detailed engineering in Brazil. The project layout is being refined as part of the basic engineering process to further reduce the overall project footprint.
  • Project debt and equity funding pathways continue to be advanced to support FID.

Centaurus strengthened its growth pipeline with the acquisition of the highly prospective Rio Novo tenure, located adjacent to the Company’s Boi Novo Copper-Gold Project in northern Brazil.

Alliance Nickel [ASX:AXN] (formerly GME Resources)

On January 28 Alliance Nickel announced:

December 2025. Quarterly activities report.

  • Appointment of Cohen & Company Capital Markets and Welsbach Corporate Solutions as advisors to explore potential US NASDAQ listing via SPAC transaction.
  • Stellantis offtake agreement to be renegotiated on terms reflecting revised project timeline.

Sama Resources [TSXV: SME] [GR;8RS] (SAMMF)

The Samapleu-Grata Nickel-Copper Project is a 60/40 joint venture between Ivanhoe Electric and Sama Resources.

No news for the month.

Murchison Minerals [TSXV:MUR] (MURMF)

No news for the month.

Giga Metals Corp. [TSXV:GIGA][FSE: BRR2] (GIGGF) Turnagain Nickel-Cobalt Project is now held via the JV company Hard Creek Nickel Corporation [TSXV:HNC] (HNCKF) (85% Giga Metals: 15% Mitsubishi Corp.)

On January 14 Giga Metals announced:

Arca and Giga Metals sign exclusive agreement to explore carbon removal at B.C. nickel project with potential to remove 220 million tonnes of atmospheric carbon dioxide...The agreement grants Arca an exclusive right to evaluate the potential of the ultramafic waste rock and mine tailings at the Turnagain Project to permanently remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.

Power Metallic Mines Inc. [TSXV:PNPN] (CMETF)

On January 19 Power Metallic Mines announced:

Power Metallic reports Lion Zone recoveries of 98.9% copper, 93.9% palladium, 96.8% platinum, 85% gold and 88.9% silver from initial metallurgical results as reported by SGS Canada Inc.

Garibaldi Resources [TSXV:GGI] [GR:RQM] [LN:OUX6] (GGIFF)

No news for the month.

The Metals Company (TMC)

On January 21 TMC announced:

TMC welcomes NOAA rule modernizing deep-seabed mining permits for U.S. companies in the high seas...New framework allows applicants to rely on exploration-phase environmental, geological, and engineering data in commercial recovery applications, reducing duplication and improving regulatory efficiency. TMC USA intends to submit a consolidated application pursuant to NOAA’s updated regulations, and anticipates that the new consolidated review process will reduce the time required to secure a commercial recovery permit.

On January 22 TMC announced:

TMC USA files first consolidated deep-seabed mining application, increasing expected commercial recovery permit area to 65,000 km2.

Other juniors

Alaska Energy Metals Corp. [TSXV:AEMC] (AKEMF), Artemis Resources [ASV:ARV], Australian Mines [ASX:AUZ], Azure Minerals [ASX:AZS] (AZRMF), Blackstone Minerals [ASX:BSX], Cassini Resources [ASX: CZI], Class 1 Nickel and Technologies Ltd. [CSE:NICO] (NICLF), Electra Battery Materials [TSXV:ELBM] (ELBMF), Electric Royalties [TSXV:ELEC], EV Nickel [TSXV:EVNI] (EVNIF), First Atlantic Nickel [TSXV:FAN] (FANCF), Flying Nickel Mining Corp. [TSXV:FLYN], FPX Nickel [TSXV:FPX], Grid Metals Corp [TSXV:GRDM], Go Metals [CSE:GOCO] (GOCOF), Huntsman Exploration [TSXV:HMAN] (BBBMF), Inomin Mines [TSXV:MINE], New Age Metals [TXV:NAM], Nickel Creek Platinum [TSX:NCP] (OTCQX:NCPCF), Nordic Nickel Limited [ASX:NNL], Pancontinental Resources Corporation [TSXV:PUC], Polymet Mining [TSX:POM], Renforth Resources [CSE:RFR] (RFHRF), Rox Resources [ASX:RXL], S2 Resources (ASX:S2R), Stillwater Critical Minerals [TSXV:PGE] (OTCQB:PGEZF), Sunrise Energy Metals [ASX:SRL] (SREMF), Surge Battery Metals Inc. [TSXV:NILI] [FRA:DJ5C] (NILIF), Talisman Mining Ltd. [ASX:TLM], Tartisan Nickel Corp. [CSE:TN] (TTSRF), Transition Metals [TSXV:XTM], URU Metals Ltd. [LSE:URU] [GR:NVRA], Wall Bridge Mining [TSX:WM], WIN Metals Ltd [ASX:WIN], and Zeb Nickel Corp. [TSXV:ZBNI] (ZBNIF). First Atlantic Nickel Corp (FANCF)

Note: Some of the above companies are covered in the Cobalt monthly news.

Nickel miners ETF

Below is the Sprott Nickel Miners ETF. You can view the top holdings here.

Sprott Nickel Miners ETF (NIKL) - Five year price chart - Price = US$19.60

Conclusion

Nickel spot prices were significantly higher the last month.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Nickel spikes more than 10% as Chinese buying fuels metals surge.
  • Fastmarkets: Nickel prices surge on fears of Indonesian mining quotas.
  • S&P Global Energy CERA projects that the nickel market will remain in surplus by 179,000mt through 2026, a deficit of 2,000 mt by 2032.
  • Indonesia cuts 2026 nickel quota, global prices jump immediately. Indonesia cuts 2026 nickel ore production quota by ~34% to 250–260 million tonnes.
  • Nornickel 2025 consolidated nickel output decreased 3% to 199 kt. Nickel output guidance for 2026 is 193-203 kt.
  • Vale Indonesia halts nickel mining on delayed work plan approval.
  • Glencore produced 71.9kt of nickel in 2025, guidance for 2026 is 70-80kt.
  • Sherritt International MOA JV (100% basis) 2025 nickel production was 25,240t at the lower ends of their revised 2025 guidance.
  • IGO - Nova mine production and costs continue to track favourably to the remaining life of mine plan.
  • Nickel Industries to acquire additional 2% interest in ENC Project, increasing its shareholding from 44% to 46%, becoming the largest shareholder in the project.
  • Talon Metals completes 1 for 10 share consolidation. Completes Eagle Mine and Humboldt Mill Operations acquisition.
  • NexMetals drills 32.45 metres of 4.61% CuEq. at Selebi North.
  • Canada Nickel announced a 46% increase in M&I Resource to 2.1 million tonnes contained nickel (0.87 billion tonnes @ 0.23% Ni) at Reid Nickel Sulphide Project.
  • Ardea Resources - The KNP-Goongarrie Hub Project DFS remains on schedule for completion in the first half of CY2026.
  • Alliance Nickel to explore potential US NASDAQ listing via SPAC transaction.
  • Power Metallic reports Lion Zone recoveries of 98.9% copper, 93.9% palladium, 96.8% platinum, 85% gold and 88.9% silver.
  • TMC USA files first consolidated deep-seabed mining application, increasing expected commercial recovery permit area to 65,000 km2.

As usual all comments are welcome.

data.title: Thrivent Global Stock Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Management

Kurt Lauber, CFA

Senior Portfolio Manager

Industry: 1990

Fund: 2013

Noah Monsen, CFA

Senior Portfolio Manager

Industry: 2008

Fund: 2018

Lauri Brunner

Senior Portfolio Manager

Industry: 1993

Fund: 2018

David Spangler, CFA

Head of Mixed Assets & Market Strategies

Industry: 1989

Fund: 2019

Executive Summary

  • For the latest quarter, the Fund (AALGX) very modestly underperformed its public benchmark but outperformed the Morningstar peer group. The overweight to equity and tilt to large cap tech contributed positively, in addition to managers in aggregate.
  • For the 1-year period, the Fund underperformed the public benchmark while outperforming the Morningstar peer group. The overweight to equity and tilt to large cap tech contributed positively, in addition to managers in aggregate.

Performance factors

For the quarter, the Fund trailed its public benchmark, the MSCI ACWI Index, by -0.11%, and outperformed its Morningstar peer group by approximately 0.69%. An overweight to equities added to performance with strong markets in the fourth quarter. Within equity, the underweight to international detracted with international leading domestic by about 2%. The underweight to mid caps added somewhat with mid caps underperforming both large and small caps. On balance, managers outperformed their individual benchmarks, with the strongest relative results from Large Cap Value, followed by Large Cap Growth. Conversely, underperforming managers included International, Mid Cap Growth and Small Cap Growth. From a market factor perspective, the Fund's growth tilt detracted from performance, however the Fund benefited from overweights to large cap tech.

Over the trailing one year, the Fund underperformed its public benchmark, the MSCI ACWI Index, by approximately - 1.50%, but outperformed its Morningstar peer group by approximately 1.26%. The overweight to equity added to performance with both domestic and international markets significantly outperforming. Overweight large caps and underweight to small and mid (SMID) caps added to performance with large caps outperforming by more than 5% to 7%. Market factor exposure to growth continued to contribute positively with growth outperforming value by more than 3%, despite a strong fourth quarter from value. On the manager level, performance was additive, with very strong results from Large Cap Value, followed by Large Cap Growth. Underperforming managers included International, Mid Cap Growth and Small Cap Growth. In the last quarter, we reduced equity three times back to target overweight levels of about 1%.

Portfolio outlook

In the U.S., the S&P 500 Index outperformed mid- and small-cap indices by roughly 5–7%. International equities were even stronger, as the MSCI EAFE Index and emerging markets indices outpaced the S&P 500 Index by approximately 14% and 16%, respectively. Nevertheless, market concentration has reached its levels not seen since the dot-com era, and valuations are elevated across most sectors. These dynamics leave equities more exposed to potential earnings disappointments, slower monetization of AI initiatives, or renewed trade and tariff pressures. While the largest U.S. companies remain highly profitable, their immense scale may limit incremental growth.

Looking ahead, we expect the U.S. economy to stay resilient, supported by pro-cyclical spending initiatives under the July 2025 tax law and further, measured rate cuts by the Federal Reserve. These conditions should continue to underpin market performance while encouraging broader participation beyond large-cap technology. International equities could also benefit from improving growth and a potentially weaker U.S. dollar.

In 2025, developed and emerging markets outperformed the U.S. primarily due to currency effects. We maintain an underweight position in developed international equities, reflecting structural headwinds such as slower growth, aging demographics, heavy regulation, and weaker innovation relative to the U.S. and Asia. Europe's relative lag in AI adoption underscores these challenges. Conversely, we are modestly overweight emerging markets, which benefit from greater technology exposure, stronger trade momentum led by China, and increased support for AI investment.

Late in 2025, small caps briefly outperformed as enthusiasm for Federal Reserve easing grew, but leadership quickly reverted to higher-quality large caps by mid-October. Given their persistent disadvantages in scale, margins, and earnings growth, we remain underweight small caps over the intermediate and long term.

data.title: Ovintiv: 2 (Big) Steps Remain
data.description:
Ovintiv Inc. (OVV) has spent years unwinding the mess created under prior CEO Doug Suttles, a business directive that was biased towards multi-basin diversification and scale at all costs. Granted, that shotgun-style approach to growth was not unusual during the early days of shale, but Ovintiv stuck to that methodology far longer than it should have, and with it at one time having exposure to six different basins, it has taken time to properly dispose of these holdings. Despite what I would call decent to good results within its drilling programs, it's been hard to be bullish for much of the past five years because there was so much to be done.

We're near the end now. The NuVista acquisition ($2,700mm) is a few days from stated closing, and if the company can execute on a planned sale of its Anadarko assets in 2026, what is left is a "Midland plus Montney" shale story, certainly two of the most premier drilling locales in North America. Debt load post-divestiture should be quite manageable, and I think the market might be apt to come around to Ovintiv if they can start to move their performance on drilling upward. While not bullish quite yet, I think Ovintiv is getting to the point where it might deserve a more discerning look from investors.

NuVista Energy Deal

I was not bullish on NuVista Energy before this acquisition, and I think that's telling, given NuVista wells in the Montney were outperforming legacy Ovintiv acreage in the area by around 15.0% on recent vintages. That acquired land interlocks geographically and operationally, filling gaps across Pipestone, Wapiti, and Karr, all while extending the footprint in the Paramount out to the west. So this isn't new rock risk, and arguably Ovintiv is (hopefully) picking up some talent that knows how to execute along this corridor better than they have been able to recently. It also comes with the added bonus of more gas processing capability on a pro forma basis, and NuVista also had much better down-market diversification on its gas sales, with a large part of its gas marketed in premium markets like Chicago, Dawn, or even priced based on JKM Asian LNG versus being sold at AECO market prices. To be clear, the Montney is an oil-directed basin; the price of oil is largely what dictates the decision to drill, despite being only 25% of the overall yield on a barrel of energy ("boe") basis. But getting better pricing on natural gas is basically free money from a producer standpoint, so it's just good business practice.

Perhaps the underappreciated part of this deal is that it comes with growth optionality. Before the buyout, NuVista's growth plan involved moving volumes from 90,000 boe/d to 125,000 boe/d by 2028, which was underwritten by 35,000 boe/d of gas processing capacity coming online (15,000 boe/d at Pipestone, which is now online, plus two 10,000 boe/d expansions at Wapiti in 2026/2027). I expect we will see dialogue around this change with crude oil back to $70.00 per barrel, and especially if we see the Venezuela/Canada heavy barrel dialogue shift back to a Canadian focus. For now, the pitch has been that consolidating interlocked acreage, wells can be drilled more efficiently and high-graded, but the 2026 Plan could surprise to the upside of drilling.

Much of NuVista was undeveloped, but even still, at the current drilling pace, we're talking fifteen to twenty years of inventory. That is 600 derisked locations plus another 300 locations that sit on multiple benches. I think part of the pitch on a Permian plus Montney-focused company is that Canadian inventories are so healthy, and having both really helps settle market concerns over Ovintiv's ability to be a large producer into the 2030s. The Montney still needs delineation and exploration to prove out the story more, but directionally it extends runway in a liquids-rich system that can compete with the Permian on returns.

Synergies Are Tangible, Perhaps Guided High

Ovintiv is calling for $100mm in annual free cash flow synergies, split evenly between capital savings and work out in the field. I think that number makes sense for NuVista, only assuming there is a lot of duplicative overhead and a method to improve capital efficiency, a number more in the $50mm range would be typical for "clean" bolt-ons where there is less to cut, and the seller was already efficient.

Capital savings are roughly $1mm per well, stemming from streamlined facility design, repeatable pad layouts, and faster cycle times. What we're talking about is basically a more unified recipe for completions across more drilling activity from the combined entity. As already mentioned, NuVista has been underperforming in the Montney versus NuVista, so I'm always skeptical that the underachieving firm can ratchet up the speed and cut costs while keeping the type curves the same. The $50mm in cost savings (more negotiating power with suppliers, lower overhead, etc.) seems far more likely, but I see more risk to missing targets versus exceeding them—at least while keeping well performance flat. This is something I'm watching closely.

Anadarko Sale: The Last Complexity Overhang

The Anadarko divestiture planned for 2026 is the other half of this story. Until it is resolved, the market will treat it as an overhang—especially because, as we talked about in Devon Energy coverage (see "Devon Energy: Recent Recap, Exploring Coterra Energy Merger"), there is another large asset package that will likely be up for sale in Anadarko. The basin is structurally extremely illiquid. Yes, there is PDP value, remaining inventory, and good infrastructure, but most public large caps have exited (ConocoPhillips (COP), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY), Marathon Oil, others). What is left is a handful of private equity-backed platforms, mostly, so getting multi-billion dollar deals done here is going to require extremely steep discounts to PDP value and potentially splitting the assets among multiple orders. Finding a single buyer to cash a check just really is not likely to happen.

This is another question mark for Ovintiv. Ovintiv not only has the question of whether management can drive improvement in its Canadian well designs thanks to the intellectual and proprietary capital from NuVista, but also at what price it can move its Anadarko lands. It has committed publicly to a 2026 sale, and that timeline narrows the buying pool further. I worry that this ends up facing the same issues Ovintiv has had in the past on selling its now non-core acreage: price.

If management executes here, Ovintiv becomes much easier to own: two core assets, lower leverage, and a clean capital return path. But that is only if it pulls this off. Historically, I've loved owning companies during periods of disruption, but I don't fully trust this one.

Conclusion

Ovintiv is finally approaching the point where the story could finally be a simple one. The NuVista deal could be a meaningful step forward if the promised synergies materialize without sacrificing well performance and if Canadian execution genuinely improves rather than just gets talked up. But the Anadarko sale remains the last real test. Until that asset is gone—and gone at a price that doesn’t feel like capitulation—Ovintiv will carry a credibility discount. If management executes cleanly on both fronts, the stock likely deserves a rerating. If they stumble on either, this risks becoming yet another chapter of “almost there”. I’m not bullish yet, but for the first time in years, Ovintiv is close enough that it warrants paying attention again.

data.title: OceanaGold: Entering The Sweet Spot
data.description:
Since I last reported on OceanaGold (OCANF) in September, the stock has soared from $20.38 to $37.90. Impressive 3Q earnings and the inexorable rise of the hallowed yellow metal explain the price climb, but the question now is whether this ticker will see further appreciation or whether it is ripe for profit taking.

My short answer: “don’t sell -- you ain’t seen anything yet.” OCANF’s 4Q will be a banner quarter. The macro picture for gold remains opportune, and, with the price topping $5499 this week, that profound tailwind remains.

In addition, I ask investors to look deeper at a host of very “company-specific” reasons – the operational setup at two mines, the buyback regime, the recent approval at Waihi North, even the doubling of its annual dividend in 2025-- which make OceanaGold worth keeping in your portfolio.

In a December interview, CEO Gerard Bond, a 29-year veteran of the mining industry, stated that the third quarter had been tapped to be the softest production quarter of the year due to a few necessary stripping campaigns, which now allowed the firm to get to the ore at two mines. In contrast, he now expects the coming 4Q to be a “strong production” quarter with a far better All-In Sustaining Cost ("AISC"), with this operational leverage continuing into the coming year.

Even at the current gold price, FY 2026 could be “shoot the lights out” good.

The Company

Formed in 1989 and headquartered in Vancouver, OceanaGold has global gold reserves of 6.2 Moz and M&I resources of 8.9 Moz, operationally diversified over four mines (three of which are in Tier 1 jurisdictions). The company began with one mine in Macraes on the South Island of New Zealand. That operation (100% company owned) has produced over 5 million ounces of Au over the course of its 36-year history. Since then, OCANF has broadened its purview to three additional locations:

  • Haile Gold Mine, US: Starting production in early 2017, this South Carolina mine only produces gold (2.72 Moz) and recently transitioned to open pit and underground operations, employing 700.
  • Didipio Mine, The Philippines: 80% owned and dug out by the company in 2013 as an open pit before it went to underground status six years late, this mine employs over 800 employees and produces gold (1.10 Moz) as well as copper.
  • Waihi Gold Mine, NZ: 100% company owned since 2015, this North Island mine produces gold and silver.

Financials:

OceanaGold delivered its latest 3Q on November 5th and it was impressive.

Quarterly revenue came in at a record $449 million, up 29.9% year over year, and it beat estimates by $36 million. This was supported by a “record average realized” gold price of $3,476 per ounce (with no hedges or prepays).

Net profit was $87 million, with an EPS of $0.38, an EBITDA Margin of 46%, and an Operating Cash Flow Per Share of $0.93.

Free Cash Flow came in at $94 million for the quarter, with a $283 million YTD that outpaces full year 2024. This gives the firm a trailing 12-month FCF yield of 15%. Not too shabby!

The firm’s cash balance popped 12% to $335 million (with no debt). The Share buyback program for the year was increased to $175 million (with $100 million having been repurchased YTD by 11/5/25).

Operationally, the company produced 103,500 ounces of gold and 3,100 tons of copper in 3Q, which was down from the 119,500 ounces of gold and 3,700 tons of copper in 2Q. This had been planned as waste stripping at Haile and Macraes was needed to get to higher-grade ore.

More importantly for our discussion, management said that this will position both sites to deliver a strong 4Q. The All-In Sustaining Cost ("AISC") of $2,052 per ounce YTD is expected to be lower in 4Q.

At present, FY 2025 guidance (which includes the remaining 4Q) is for 450 – 620 koz /13 -15 kt of copper in production at a $1,900 - $2,050 AISC. New 2026 guidance is to be issued in February --probably during the Feb. 18th call--and will include updated technical reports for at least three mines.

Organic Drivers and New Catalysts for 2026:

During the last earnings call, management mentioned more exploration success at Haile, suggesting direct, organic growth from that US mine.

The plan is to optimize and extend Horseshoe Underground and pull its first ore from Palomino Underground in 2028. Ledbetter Phase 4 and Pisces are sited for their potential future underground ore source. Haile has been traditionally blessed with a low $24 discovery cost per ounce.

Operationally, OceanaGold said it was on track to unlock higher grade ore at Haile and Macraes starting in the second half of the year.

Throughout most of 2025, OceanaGold was focused on waste stripping at Haile’s Ledbetter Phase 3 (hurting total ore tons mined). Now the firm expects serious 4Q ore grade improvements. Grade-driven production growth, higher O/P and UG grades will come in stronger in 2026, with a 60% year-over-year growth expected.

In terms of Macraes, the firm stated that it expected a much better Q4 tally. 3Q waste stripping will lead to better access to Innes Mills Phase 8 ore. This mine saw its AISC drop from $2,319 in 2Q 2024 to $2,146 in 2Q 2025 and that should continue.

More dramatically, OCANF got a fast-track permit approval for the Waihi North Project on December 18th. I see this as a new catalyst and a long-term win, extending the value of OceanaGold shares deep into the 2030s.

The company has lauded the site “one of the best undeveloped, high-grade ore bodies in our industry.” The permit includes the development of Wharekirauponga (i.e. the Waihi north part) and the associated surface infrastructure, an expansion of the current processing and water treatment plants as well as new construction of a tailings storage unit. The permit ups the number of exploration drill pads and a doubling of the allowable drill rigs to six, which should allow OCANF to accelerate its definition and conversion drilling at Wharekirauponga next year.

This is a significant milestone for the company, with the tunneling of the underground mine expected to begin in 1H of 2026. A glance at the drilling highlights released recently by the company suggests high-grade mineralization extending at the southern boundary particularly at the WKP137A intercepts:

According to the company, Wharekirauponga Underground has a reserve grade of 9.2 g/ton. All in, the Waihi District is expected to produce a combined 1.6 million ounces of gold over the course of 15 years (2024-2038). Bravo!

Big Picture

In 2024 OceanaGold produced more than 487,000 koz of gold. The firm has reaffirmed much higher production and guided to 450 to 520 koz in 2025 and 550 to 620 koz by FY 2026, though new (probably higher) guidance for 2026 will be issued in February.

Consensus revenue for FY 2025 is $1.86 billion. The first three quarters provided $1.248 billion, and 4Q (which ended Dec. 31) must hit $619 million to reach it. I see them surpassing that number, due to operation improvements and the average gold price for 4Q 2025 being $4,135 per ounce (a 55% year-over-year increase!).

Consensus revenue for FY 2026 is $2.64 billion. This appears quite reachable with even the existing 620,000 koz production estimate and the December 2025 an ounce average, a price now well surpassed over the course of January.

If guidance is hiked to 570 – 640 koz and the full 2026 gold price average is upped to $5300, we have $3.00 - $3.4 billion in revenues. The CEO has also described a far better (i.e. lower) All-In Sustaining Cost now that the waste stripping efforts at Haile and Macraes have been resolved, so expect better profits.

Remember: Haile provides 38% and Macraes 29% of OCANF’s annual production, respectively. With the gloves off, those two mines provide a lot of torque. Also: a $5300 full year 2026 gold price average is rather conservative, as it has already been surpassed in the first four weeks of a 52 week year.

Risks:

The risks to owning OceanaGold are myriad. Mining operations are fraught with danger and serious hazards that can set back production for years. More broadly, the precious metals trade is now “long in the tooth.” The concurrent silver trade appears frothy and overheated. There is a real possibility that the price of gold could go sideways or fall over the course of 2026 if swing traders flee the trade for greener pastures. Maybe the sovereign debt time bomb is defused for now and the fiat currencies just shuffle on. Maybe AI productivity really does “square the circle” and turbocharge economic growth without decimating the white collar middle class and triggering a long recession.

In the very short term, gold is not the hedge you think it is. A black swan liquidity event in the financial markets will pull down gold as fast as other assets as people pull money from where ever they still have it. Gold only rises back up when governments open their purse strings.

With a P/FCF of 9.68 and a P/E (GAAP) of 21.92, OCANF is not as cheap as it was when I pounded the table in September. Back then the company had a $4.4 billion market cap; now it is $8.45 billion. Nevertheless, I see a convergence of good news keeping OceanaGold in the sweet spot over the next 12 months.

Management will release 4Q earnings on February 18, 2026 and probably use the opportunity to provide new technical reports. I am looking forward to it.

data.title: SEIV: Multi-Factor Active Large-Cap ETF With A 12x P/E Continues To Shine
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the SEI Enhanced US Large Cap Value ETF (SEIV), which continues to deliver impressive returns and remains one of the top-ranked large-cap value funds in my fundamentals-driven ETF scoring system. As I discussed in November, SEIV isn't a single-factor fund, and that's evident when evaluating its fundamentals, as it has above-average exposure to the quality, momentum, and sentiment factors. You also won't find many other funds trading at just 12.29x forward earnings, so in short, SEIV has a lot going for it, and that's why I've decided to assign it a "buy" rating today. Please enjoy the read, and as always, I welcome your questions in the comments section afterward.

SEIV Overview

My review in November was education-themed, but the main takeaway from a strategy perspective was that SEIV is not a single-factor fund, which I think is crucial to being able to succeed in multiple market environments. There are plenty of large-cap value funds for you to choose from - 149 in my database, to be exact. However, of the ones with ultra-cheap P/E ratios, you'll normally see significant tradeoffs on other factors. I've listed the ten cheapest in the table below plus some relevant fundamental statistics as of January 24, 2026, and this view quickly highlights how SEIV stands out in a good way.

Most ETFs fail the growth test, as they currently own stocks that, in aggregate, have negative earnings growth over the last three years. For the ones that do a little better on growth, you'll notice that their margins are on the low side, they lack exposure to the momentum factor, or their expense ratios are far too high. In contrast, SEIV strikes a nice balance on all measures. Its 0.15% expense ratio is the second-lowest in this group, its 3.86% 3Y E​PS CAGR demonstrates some earnings durability, its 19.53% E​BIT margins are above average, and its 27.42% one-year total return is the best in this group. In fact, SEIV finished 2025 as the second-best-performing large-cap value ETF behind the iShares MSCI USA Value Factor ETF (VLUE), and its three-year total return through December 2025 ranks #4/95.

The SEIV website and my educational article contain additional information about its strategy, but the most important takeaway for readers is to treat it as a multi-factor fund. Again, I think this approach is smart and, in a certain way, humble. If I knew which factor the market was going to favor each year, I could easily identify the right ETFs to buy. However, that's not realistic, so the next best thing is to merely increase your chances of success in any environment by ensuring no factor is left behind.

SEIV Performance

Before we move into the evidence of SEIV's multi-factor approach in action, I want to quickly touch on its performance, as that could also help identify potential strengths and weaknesses in its strategy. History is limited, since SEIV only launched on May 18, 2022, but results from June 2022 to December 2025 were excellent. The performance statistics table below also includes other top performers over this period, including the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV), the Putnam Focused Large Cap Value ETF (PVAL), and the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV). As a benchmark, I'll also profile the SPDR S&P 500 Value ETF (SPYV).

SEIV's 15.98% annualized return wasn't the best, but just as important were its inferior risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino ratios) and "worst 12 months" figures compared to CGDV and PVAL. In fairness, drawdowns were limited during this 3.5-year period, so this might not be the best way to assess downside risk. However, I think it's something we need to watch for, and I'll offer some insights shortly based on its fundamentals and composition.

I also want to point out that SEIV's relatively poor downside risk-adjusted returns were limited to 2022. As summarized below, the fund declined by 11.5% and 10.0% in June and September 2022, finishing up that partial year with an 8.53% total loss. Since then, returns have been steady, with its 27.42% total return in 2025 doubling up on SPYV.

SEIV Analysis

SEIV Sector Allocations vs. Peer Group

We can partially attribute SEIV's success to date to its tech sector exposure, currently at 30.51%. This is 9.93% more than the group average, and SEIV also overweights Communication Services by 6.86%. Top holdings in this sector include Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Verizon Communications (VZ), AT&T (T), and Fox Corp. (FOXA).

In contrast, SEIV underweights Energy and Industrials by 4.85% and 5.83%, respectively. Underweighting energy is a great example of the discipline a multi-factor approach forces. To illustrate, the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) currently trades with an 18.33x forward P/E, the third-cheapest S&P 500 Index sector ETF. However, its margin and capital efficiency ratios are low, and its 3Y E​PS C​AGR is -15.90%, reflecting how difficult it is to forecast earnings in commodity-driven sectors. Limiting exposure to this sector aligns with my long-term outlook that favors stability, and I also think it aligns well with the conservative nature of many value investors.

SEIV Fundamental Analysis

The next table highlights selected fundamental statistics for SEIV's top 20 holdings, which comprised 45.79% of the fund as of January 24, 2026. Please have a scan of these and the summary statistics at the bottom, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. SEIV's three-year portfolio beta of 1.03 is somewhat high for a large-cap value fund. While beta only measures market risk, this figure, coupled with the higher standard deviation figures highlighted earlier, suggests SEIV probably isn't the best fund for investors focused primarily on risk management. SEIV's 2.00% Index yield, which I estimate will net investors 1.85% after factoring in the fund's 0.15% expense ratio, also isn't very high, but I think it's solid for a multi-factor fund.

2. SEIV's forward and trailing P/E ratios are 12.29x and 15.62x, respectively, which are substantially lower than the four other ETFs listed. It's interesting that four funds can produce similar results using different strategies, and that opens up the door to owning several of these ETFs to effectively differentiate by manager. SEIV's overlap with CGDV, PVAL, and FDVV ranges between 12% and 19%, and the added diversification from this approach should reduce some of the risk identified in point #1.

3. SEIV has two advantages right now. The first is excellent momentum factor exposure, as its constituents' share prices are up 23.44% over the last six months compared to only 5-11% for the other four ETFs. This is further supported by its components' aggregate 11.38% earnings surprise, which I use as a key input in sentiment analysis. The second is a huge differential between its components' three-year E​PS C​AGR and what analysts currently estimate for E​PS growth over the next twelve months. I call this "investing in turnaround stocks," and it's a common characteristic in many funds I follow. As shown, PVAL is executing this strategy nicely right now (0.83% vs. 9.42%), as is VLUE, the top fund in the category last year.

While I can't say for certain that this strategy is intentional, it is a unique way to promote high-momentum stocks. My only caution is that low-single-digit or negative historical E​PS growth could signal an earnings durability problem where P/E ratios could collapse in an earnings shock. However, SEIV's 3.86% rate isn't too bad - it ranks #95/149 large-cap value ETFs I track. I expect it could still be problematic if market conditions worsen, but I'd also expect its active managers to rotate into more stable stocks. Unfortunately, I don't know if that will happen, given the fund's short history, but in that environment, funds like the WisdomTree U.S. Dividend Growth ETF (DGRW) are probably better bets.

4. Value stocks simply are lower-quality than growth stocks at the moment, and that's easily backed up by fundamental statistics like E​BIT margins, net margins, and return on equity. However, when isolating the analysis only to large-cap value ETFs, SEIV looks slightly better than average. Its E​BIT and net margins of 19.53% and 15.01% are low, but its capital efficiency ratios (R​OE and R​OTC) are still competitive, especially for the value (P/E) offered.

Portfolio X-Ray

In my second point, I suggested owning multiple ETFs as a way to diversify by manager. I encourage you to experiment with your actual holdings using my Portfolio X-Ray Tool, but hypothetically, if you were to own SEIV, CGDV, PVAL, FDVV, and the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) in equal weight, you could mitigate several of the issues I identified today. Consider these results:

The net result (bottom row) highlights how:

  • Portfolio beta remains unchanged at 1.03.
  • E​BIT margins and return on total capital improve.
  • Historical and estimated earnings growth rates increase.
  • The historical and estimated earnings growth differential declines.

You'll have to decide if the increase in forward P/E (17.85x vs. 12.29x) is worth it, but in my view, it is. We've already established that these ETFs can deliver similarly high returns without ultra-low valuations, but the key is to recognize in advance that eventually, one of these approaches will fall out of favor. Paying more via a higher P/E to improve your portfolio's fundamentals acts as an additional layer of insurance beyond what SEIV already provides with its multi-factor approach, and this hypothetical portfolio's net 17.85x forward P/E is still very much in value territory, given how the average for the large-cap value category is only a point lower at 16.87x.

Investment Recommendation

This article highlighted SEIV's multi-factor approach to stock selection, which I believe is responsible for its success since its launch in May 2022. My factor analysis revealed strengths in momentum, sentiment, and especially value, given its 12.29x forward P/E ratio. SEIV's growth and quality metrics were also adequate, and based on my knowledge of other ETFs, I'm confident that a hidden advantage is how much higher its components' estimated E​PS growth rates are compared to its historical E​PS growth rates. SEIV's holdings don't necessarily have the stability and consistency compared to those held by ETFs like DGRW, but this strategy works well in risk-on environments, and I expect continued success in 2026 if earnings surprises stay above average.

I don't recommend owning SEIV as your sole large-cap value ETF. The fund is just too new, and it hasn't been tested in a market correction. However, that shouldn't prevent you from considering it as part of a broader portfolio of ETFs. This article highlighted three others to complement SEIV and SPY, a core fund I believe most investors should own. By holding these funds in equal weight, investors would still end up with a value-oriented portfolio with the same 1.03 five-year beta and improved fundamentals. Specifically, it reduces the risk of an earnings shock, in which markets won't emphasize earnings growth history rather than potentially lofty expectations. It's one example of how you can use fundamental analysis to get the exposure that best fits your needs, but regardless, I find SEIV's cheap P/E and average-or-better exposure to other factors unique, so I'm comfortable assigning it a "buy" rating today. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Microsoft Meltdown Fuels AI Fears
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Almost all the line items in Microsoft Corp.’s (MSFT) fiscal second quarter looked great. But the stock melted down the most since 2020 anyway. Why? It all goes back to worries about AI spending.

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day, which shows Microsoft stock going back 12 months. You can see that MSFT was already losing momentum, with RSI peaking last summer and the shares making a series of lower highs in Q4 2025. But yesterday’s breakdown on massive volume really stands out!

So, what happened? Let’s start with the positives from Microsoft's report. Profit came in at $38.5 billion, or $5.16 per share. That beat analyst estimates. Revenue came in at $81.3 billion. That also beat estimates. Operating income? Same story.

But here's the thing: Capital expenditures soared 66% year-over-year to $37.5 billion. At the same time, sales growth at its Azure cloud computing division slowed just a bit to 38%.

That dredged up lingering concerns that Big Tech is spending oodles of cash on data centers, Artificial Intelligence model development, and human capital…but that all of the upfront investment might not generate enough profit down the road. While Meta Platforms Inc. (META) also reported results – and did just fine – the net effect was for tech stocks to slide. The State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK) is now basically unchanged since October.

Clearly, the AI story isn’t going away. But neither are AI spending worries. Keep a close eye on this leadership group – because if it continues to struggle, the markets probably will, too.
