data.title: Bank of Canada On Hold: What Comes Next For Rates And The Loonie
data.description:
The Bank of Canada left rates unchanged as expected, but uncertainty around tariffs, the U.S. dollar, and the next policy move remains. Francis Fong, Senior Economist with TD Bank Group, breaks down what the decision signals and the key indicators to watch in the months ahead.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie - The Bank of Canada left its overnight interest rate unchanged at 2.25% after six consecutive cuts. Joining me now to discuss the latest decision is Francis Fong, Senior Economist at TD Bank Group. And Francis, what's your reaction to the decision? Any surprises here?

Francis Fong - Thanks for having me, Anthony. So yeah, no real surprises here. I think it was widely expected that the bank was going to leave rates unchanged. I think what's key here, though, is to look at the monetary policy report that they released alongside their rate decision to see how their view of the outlook has evolved over the past few months.

And there also, no surprises as well. I think, overall, the Canadian economy has shown more resilience than we would have given it credit for about a year ago. A lot of the main indicators are sort of showing signs of, one, resilience, and then two, things heading in the right direction. So inflation, it's still elevated, but the core measures are moving in the right direction, with two of the three core CPI measures heading closer and closer to that 2% target.

So that's a positive. If we look at things like trade and the labor market, again, as to be expected, there's a lot of weakness there. But again, we're still seeing more resilience than we would have given it credit for a year ago.

Anthony Okolie - So given the resilience in the economy, do you think the Bank of Canada is likely to keep interest rates on hold for the rest of the year? Or could we even potentially see a rate hike at some point?

Francis Fong - Yeah, so the rate hike conversation has been quite strong in recent months. And I think a lot of it is due to the way the inflation numbers have evolved over the last few months. We've seen things like food inflation come up quite a bit. And that's being driven by all sorts of factors - trade and tariffs, climate change leading to droughts all across the world, and things of that nature.

So we're seeing things like beef prices really high, coffee prices really high. We're still seeing rent inflation to be quite sticky. But if we look through those things, and this is what the Bank of Canada is likely to do, they'll look through these transitional factors; if we look at some of these core measures or factors that were contributing to high inflation previously, things like owned accommodation inflation and a lot of the core measures, they are headed in that right direction.

So we don't think that the hike conversation is really justified, especially when we're anticipating that growth is likely to remain weak over the medium term. So we have the Bank sort of staying pat for the remainder of the year.

Anthony Okolie - All right, now the big question, of course, remains tariffs and the lack of a deal with the United States. What happens if we don't reach a deal? And what could that mean for monetary policy going forward?

Francis Fong - Yeah, this is a tough question to answer. Obviously, the geopolitical situation is quite volatile. We just saw sort of Trump announce -- or a new threat around a new 100% tariff, which remains to be seen whether it will actually get implemented.

The important thing here, I think, is to sort of look through all of this to see what's actually in place today. And if you look at what's been announced versus what the actual effective tariff rate is, it's quite a bit lower than what's actually been announced.

The effective tariff rate, according to the Bank of Canada, which they've assessed, the amount of duties that are being collected by the US on Canadian exports is actually around 6%. And the effective tariff rate that Canada is applying to the US is about 1%. So beyond all of the talk about it, the actual effective tariff rate is quite a bit lower.

Now, were there to be a trade deal, a new USMCA, it's being renegotiated this year; the US is likely to trigger that renegotiation early. All of that would alleviate a lot of this uncertainty that we're facing. So for the time being, I think we just have to wait and see what actually comes out of these negotiations.

But if we look at the underlying trend, we're not seeing trade decline by quite as much as, again, we thought it would, given the bluster around high tariff rates. And there's a lot of contributing factors to that. One is the tariff rate, the effective tariff rate that I mentioned earlier.

But we're seeing a lot of policy decisions from the government to move beyond that. We're seeing diversification of trade away from the US to our other trading partners. And we're seeing some benefits from things like commodity markets, where gold and silver prices - Canada mines quite a bit of that - is actually contributing to a significant increase in precious metal exports, which is offsetting some of the negative impacts faced by steel and aluminum, autos, and things of that nature. So there's things to remain positive about. But obviously, the uncertainty is going to be a huge factor.

Anthony Okolie - All right, now let's talk a little bit about the US dollar because it has had a pretty rough start against a basket of currencies. Do you think we could see more upside for the Loonie going forward?

Francis Fong - Yeah, and this is where those geopolitical risks and some of the chaos coming from the Trump administration really playing out. This broad US dollar weakness, that's been a story for the last 12 months or so.

And the Canadian dollar, despite being sort of right in the line of sight from the US administration, has benefited from that. We've seen the US dollar weakened quite significantly against the euro, about 14% against the euro, about 10% against the pound, similar against the Swiss Franc.

And so the Canadian dollar is up about 4% over the past year, which is helping to cushion some of that blow. And then we do anticipate that broad strengthening against the US dollar from all currencies to continue on. So it's at about $0.72 right now. And we're anticipating about $0.75 by the end of the year.

Anthony Okolie - All right, now finally, given all that, we've got a resilient economy. What key indicators will you be watching closely over the next few months?

Francis Fong - Yeah, Anthony, is it a little glib to say everything? Because unfortunately, we're looking at everything. We have to look at how the inflation data evolves, how much pass-through is going to come through on tariffs, how much resilience is the labor market showing, what's happening with trade, these trade deals, geopolitical risks - all of that. There's a lot going on.

And unfortunately, every single one of those factors could contribute to weakening of the economy, something happening with inflation, and financial risks as well. We saw the Japanese bond market sort of implode. That had knock-on effects to the rest of the economy. High equity valuations are also a significant risk going forward. All of these things are contributing to our outlook. And so it's unfortunate, but it is something that we have to look at.

Original Post

data.title: InfuSystem: Regulatory Backed Market Catalyst
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

It is now almost three months since my previous article on InfuSystem Holdings, Inc. (INFU). The company stock price has lost so far by around 14.52%, from $9.92 to the current $8.48, which I think the last quarter (Q3 2025) has resulted in this bearish move. Why I hold this thought is because the third quarter was the second consecutive quarter of missing analyst estimates by about -0.86%, which is higher compared to Q2 2025, which was -0.38%. In my view, Q3 2025 has resulted in weak investor confidence in the company’s performance.

However, I am still optimistic about InfuSystem, hinged on Infusion pumps. You might be concerned that in my previous article, I gave depth on Infusion pumps, but it has not yet paid off. Not that infusion pumps are not gaining traction, in fact, they are gaining traction, and I believe that with the pharmaceutical firms introducing drugs that are compatible with ambulatory infusion pumps, the pumps are going to gain more market acceptance. There is a shift towards home-based care, and InfuSystem infusion pumps are among the telemedicine equipment used remotely. The palliative care in the management of pain has now become a standard practice, and this has removed the need for hospitalization.

This gives a glimpse of the sustained demand, which I will delve into later. Still, on why I am optimistic, under the NOPAIN Act, 2 electronic infusion pumps from InfuSystem are now included in the list of qualified products for separate payments under the NOPAIN Act, which was effective as of 1st January 2026. I believe this is likely to drive this company’s revenue rebound in 2026 because the demand for infusion pumps has met government backing through the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services [CMS]. Now, the government will support the use of infusion pumps over opioids to curtail drug addiction in America.

I still believe that this is why the company’s 6-month price return at 41.36% is still beating the S&P 500, which is at 9.21%.

Now, let me delve into why I think the NOPAIN Act adds to my previous bullish view of untapped infusion demand and why this ACT tailwind warrants a bullish entry.

NOPAIN Act tailwind

I am hinged on the NOPAIN Act, including InfuSystem 2 of its infusion pumps (CADD-Solis infusion pump and Sapphire infusion pump) under Medicare and Medicaid benefits, which is now under the reach of all Americans undergoing the opioid crisis. InfuSystem uses CASS-Solis in most of its pain management services, which will make its operations more impactful. The latest data from the National Safety Council on Opioids shows that Americans are at high risk of succumbing to an opioid overdose over suicide or a car crash.

The NOPAIN Act is addressing the crisis of pain management drug overdose, which is the major driver of these drug overdoses. Over 2 million Americans are struggling with opioid disorders annually, which is a worrying trend. Again, the available data on pain management drugs shows that over 105,000 Americans die from drug overdose per year, and Opioids are leading at around 76% of all abused drugs. Opioids are a major factor in these deaths because it is estimated that about 7 Americans out of every 10 engage in overdose deaths.

Every year, about 80 million adults are prescribed pain relief medications for acute pain, and this usually lasts for around 3 months. Out of the 80 million Americans, 40 million with acute pain are prescribed opioids to manage their discomfort, which now amplifies the risk of overdose. The one issue that the NOPAIN Act is coming to reshape is the side effects associated with Opioids, which is why infusion pumps could win at this.

The side effects of using Opioids are numerous, ranging from tolerance, meaning that a patient could need more medication to address the same pain relief. Other side effects include depression, increased sensitivity to pain, confusion, and physical dependence in case of withdrawal when the medication is stopped. This is why opioids are highly associated with this crisis. Compared to infusion pumps, research shows that patients with implanted pumps using IT opioid delivery report significantly lower pain severity. With the infusion pumps, they are implanted in a way that the patient will be able to push the button and deliver pain medications directly to the spinal cord to bring about pain relief without the side effects that often come with Opioids taken by mouth. Patients will also have a psychological boost by having greater control over the pain. As I earlier provided, infusion pumps are mostly used by people experiencing chronic pain, such as in oncology, but also by patients with other types of pain.

From my explanations above, the NOPAIN Act optimism is evident, and I think this is a landmark decision. The country is now fighting to reduce the opioid epidemic by approving alternative non-opioids in pain management. Infusion pumps can be accurately done at home-based care, without developing many side effects compared to opioids. As such, this indicates InfuSystem is positioning itself with infusion pumps for the projected virtual care, which might result in 11% new patient evaluation visits and 20% patient visits conducted virtually by 2035.

Financials and Valuation

As noted previously, InfuSystem has been missing its quarterly estimates, which perhaps explains the dip since my previous article. Its revenue growth trajectory is showing some signs of weakness. Its YoY growth has decreased from 14.4% YoY in 2023 to the trailing YoY growth of 6.2%. The quarterly performance is neither an exception with revenue momentum weakening consistently over the last three quarters from 8.5% in Q1 2025 to 3.1% in Q3 2025.

The primary reason behind this weakness is the weak performance in the biomedical services. For instance, in Q3 2025, the biomedical services revenue came in at about $8.5 million, down from $9.6 million in 2024 marking a slump of about 11%. This weakening performance in the segment is attributed to weak demand.

Nevertheless, I am confident the company is likely to come back given the strong momentum seen in oncology and wound care expansion, with oncology services revenue hitting its all time high in Q3 2025. Further, the NOPAIN Act has opened a new regulatory-backed tailwind that aligns with the government’s commitment to regulate the intake of opioids.

Looking at it from a different perspective, there are over 2.5 million Americans eligible for durable medical equipment [DME]. This means that around 2.5 million Americans have access to 80% Medicare coverage for the DME supplies. Patients only pay 20%, and this is also reduced by Medicaid, Medigap, or other financial assistance programs. Therefore, including InfuSystem infusion pumps gives access to already 2.5 million Americans who may consider precision pain management over the use of opioids. In my view, I see all this conundrum rekindle the topline performance upwards.

With FY2025 facing a revenue miss, I think the company may still miss its revenue consensus estimates of $143.88 million. The FY2026 still shows a conservative revenue outlook of $148.06 million, representing 2.91% YoY growth, down from 6.69% in FY2025. But given the optimism described above, plus the gradual revenue increase, there is reason to remain optimistic about InfuSystem.

The bottom line reflects this optimism, looking at the trajectory of net income in the last three years. Since 2022, with a net income of $18,000, the company has embarked on a consistent net income increase, reaching the trailing net income of $5.52 million.

Looking ahead, the 2026 EPS estimate might hit $0.41, up from the projected $0.29 in FY2025. I think that despite a slowing YoY top line growth rate, the company’s bottom line has been resilient and consistently growing, a testament to its exceptional execution.

I will value InfuSystem by comparing its P/E multiple to that of its peer, Viemed Healthcare, Inc. (VMD). INFU’s P/E multiple is at 31.27x, down from my previous article at 41.80x. Compared to VMD’s P/E ratio, which is at 21.73x, up from 18.76x in my previous article.

To make a good evaluation, I will bring in the EPS growth metrics to give this valuation a better dimension. INFU has a trailing YoY EPS growth of 271.43%, compared to VMD’s 35.92%. More interestingly, its forward EPS growth of 117.58% compared to VMD’s 31.62% is unmatched. Looking at these figures, the obvious conclusion is that INFU deserves a premium valuation. Most importantly, I think the market is obsessed with the topline performance, punishing this company while ignoring its profitability strength. In my view, the decline in PE is set to rebound because the levers I have discussed in the article will not only sustain the profitability momentum, but also stabilize the topline growth.

Investment Risk: Uncertain Adoption and Reimbursement Sustainability

You note that in this bullish rating, I am hinged on the NOPAIN Act, and the performance embodied in this act is in its acceptance through adoption and reimbursement. The risk in this is that the Medicare payments for the infusion pumps will only take place in 2 years, that is, from 2026 to 31st December 2027. As such, the company’s forward-looking remains uncertain after 2 years regarding customer adoption and reimbursement patterns. Moreover, the programs face discontinuation after 2027, which presents a major regulatory risk. Therefore, reliance on the NOPAIN Act to drive the momentum is seemingly a short-term endeavor.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my previous bullish stance because I believe the NOPAIN Act is about to spur the demand for an additional 2 infusion pumps into the Medicare and Medicaid covers from InfuSystem. This is likely to increase the demand for infusion pumps as the government seeks to suppress the abuse of opioids.

data.title: France's Growth Stabilises At A Low Level After A Turbulent 2025
data.description:
By Charlotte de Montpellier, Senior Economist, France and Switzerland

Positive surprise in 2025

GDP growth stood at 0.2% quarter‑on‑quarter in the fourth quarter, an expected slowdown after the strong +0.5% surprise in the previous quarter. Total production of goods and services also decelerated sharply, rising by just 0.1% compared with 0.8% in the third quarter.

For the year as a whole, GDP growth averaged 0.9%, below the +1.1% recorded in 2024 but still stronger than anticipated at the beginning of last year.

The prolonged budget saga and the US trade war weighed on activity in 2025, particularly on household consumption, which increased by only 0.4% over the year, and investment, which grew by a modest 0.2% in 2025. Despite easing inflation and rising real wages, households accumulated record savings in mid‑2025, before reducing them slightly at year-end. This helped consumption regain some momentum in the final months of the year.

Some sectors surprised to the upside, notably aeronautics, while public consumption rose by a dynamic 1.7% in 2025, far outpacing GDP growth. Last year, inventories were the largest contributor to annual growth (+0.7 percentage points); strong imports and significant stockpiling of aeronautical parts in the first half were followed by export‑driven destocking in the second.

Reduced fiscal and political uncertainty is good news

At the end of January 2026, budget uncertainty finally eased with the adoption, via Article 49.3, of a compromise bill designed to avoid a no‑confidence vote. The budget includes tax increases, especially for large companies, with the tax burden set to rise to 43.9% of GDP in 2026 (43.6% in 2025). Public spending is expected to decline slightly to 56.6% of GDP, thanks to operational savings, down from 56.8% in 2025. The deficit should narrow to 5%, below 2025’s 5.4% but above the previously promised 4.6% in the structural plan submitted to EU authorities. Public debt will continue to climb, reaching 118% of GDP in 2026 and 120% in 2027, up from 110% at the start of 2024.

The European Commission’s economic sentiment indicator, which rebounded sharply in January, confirms that the end of the drawn‑out budget process has eased uncertainty and reduced the risk of snap elections. This has brought French‑German spreads to their lowest level since summer 2024, supporting a slightly more positive outlook for 2026.

However, a strong recovery is still unlikely. The budget remains unfavourable to businesses, and higher taxes could weigh on investment and job creation. With no structural challenges being addressed, the 2027 budget looks set to be even more complex – a factor likely to raise uncertainty again, push long‑term rates higher, and continue to constrain economic activity.

Growth could reach 1% in 2026

Overall, the 2026 outlook is moderately positive, with GDP growth expected to reach around 1%. Business sentiment is improving, particularly in industry, supported by the broader European rebound and the German stimulus plan. Industrial firms report better activity prospects and improving order books. Defence spending will continue to support the sector, and aeronautical exports should remain a key growth engine, provided production capacity keeps pace.

However, if it continues, the recent appreciation of the euro could weigh on exports and thus on French activity. According to the European Central Bank's latest estimates, a further 4.3% rise in the euro against the dollar subtracts 0.1 percentage points from eurozone GDP. High tax pressure may also act as a brake, and recent business surveys show investment intentions remain very weak.

Household confidence has strengthened in recent months, and reduced fears of unemployment could lower the still‑high savings rate, supporting a modest pickup in consumption in a context of sub‑2% inflation and rising real wages. Household investment should also increase slightly, even though long‑term interest rates may continue to edge up.

In 2027, growth could reach 1.1%, but political and fiscal risks remain elevated. Overall, persistent uncertainty will likely keep French growth slightly below the European average.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more
